44	# Lean ↔ Blueprint Checker
45	
46	You compare **one Lean file** against **its blueprint chapter** and report whether the Lean follows the blueprint faithfully. You are read-only.
47	
48	## Your Job
49	
50	Your directive names one `.lean` file (e.g. `Algebra/WLocal.lean`) and its corresponding blueprint chapter (e.g. `blueprint/src/chapters/Algebra_WLocal.tex`). You audit **bidirectionally**:
51	
52	### (A) Does the Lean follow the blueprint?
53	
54	For each `\lean{...}`-tagged block in the chapter, verify the Lean side:
55	
56	- **Declaration exists** with the right name (matches `\lean{namespace.theorem_name}`).
57	- **Signature matches** the informal statement in the chapter — the types are what the prose says they should be. A statement that type-checks but uses the wrong type class (e.g. `Smooth f` where the prose says "smooth of relative dimension n") is **wrong**, not "approximate".
58	- **Proof follows the blueprint sketch** — the proof in the Lean file should be a formalization of the steps in the chapter's `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}`, not a different argument that happens to produce the same conclusion.
59	- **No fake / placeholder statements** — definitions or theorems whose body is just `:= sorry` or a trivially-true tautology while the chapter prose claims something substantive.
60	- **No "temporary" definitions left in place** — comments like `-- TODO: replace with the real definition`, `-- placeholder until we figure out X`, or definitions whose body is suspicious (e.g. `def Foo := True`, weakened-wrong definitions standing in for the real concept).
61	- **No comments that explain away wrong code** — "we are using a wrong definition for now", "this proof is incomplete but will be fixed", etc. These are red flags, not acceptable workflow notes.
62	
63	### (B) Is the blueprint adequate for this Lean file?
64	
65	The blueprint can also be the failure mode. For the chapter you're reading, check:
66	
67	- **Every Lean declaration in this file should map to a blueprint block** (via `\lean{...}`) OR be an explicitly-helper-only declaration. If the file has substantive declarations the blueprint doesn't reference at all, the chapter is incomplete.
68	- **Proof sketches in the blueprint should be detailed enough** for a prover to formalize correctly. If you see Lean proofs that clearly went through significant reasoning the blueprint's `\begin{proof}` does NOT preview, flag the chapter as under-specified.
69	- **`\lean{...}` hints should pin the Lean target precisely.** If the blueprint says "smooth morphism" without specifying which Mathlib predicate (`Smooth` vs `SmoothOfRelativeDimension n` vs `IsSmooth`), and the Lean file ended up with the wrong one, that's BOTH a Lean error AND a blueprint clarity failure. Flag both sides.
70	- **Definitions in the blueprint should be at the right level of generality** for the Lean to consume. If the blueprint defines something narrower than the project actually needs and the Lean had to write a parallel API to cover the gap, that's a blueprint adequacy failure.
71	
72	Both directions matter. Don't excuse a wrong Lean statement by saying "the blueprint is loose"; don't excuse a thin blueprint by saying "the Lean works anyway." Report both.

6	import Mathlib
7	
8	/-!
9	# Stalk of a tensor product of presheaves of modules (ingredient d.2)
10	
11	This file constructs the *comparison map* underlying the varying-ring stalk--tensor
12	commutation isomorphism
13	
14	  `(A ⊗ᵖ B).stalk x ≅ A.stalk x ⊗_{R.stalk x} B.stalk x`
15	
16	for presheaves of modules over the structure presheaf `R` of a scheme — the single
17	genuinely Mathlib-absent ingredient ("d.2") on which the unconditional associator of
18	the relative-Picard tensor substrate rests.
19	
20	This iteration builds the forward additive comparison map `stalkTensorDesc` and its
21	germ characterisation (the colimit-descent of the sectionwise base-change maps). The
22	full isomorphism `PresheafOfModules.stalkTensorIso` (blueprint `lem:stalk_tensor_commutation`)
23	additionally requires the `R.stalk x`-linear packaging of this map, the reverse map, and
24	their mutual inversion on germs — see the handoff in `task_results`.
25	
26	The construction proceeds bottom-up:
27	
28	* `PresheafOfModules.stalkTensorBilin` — for a neighbourhood `U ∋ x`, the `R(U)`-balanced
29	  bilinear map `A(U) × B(U) → A_x ⊗_{R_x} B_x`, `(a,b) ↦ germ a ⊗ germ b`.
30	* `PresheafOfModules.stalkTensorDescU` — its descent through `TensorProduct.liftAddHom`
31	  to a map `(A ⊗ᵖ B)(U) = A(U) ⊗_{R(U)} B(U) → A_x ⊗_{R_x} B_x`.
32	* `PresheafOfModules.stalkTensorDesc` — the colimit descent to the comparison map
33	  `(A ⊗ᵖ B).stalk x → A_x ⊗_{R_x} B_x`.
34	
35	Source: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma `lemma-stalk-tensor-product`.
36	
37	Blueprint: `chapters/Picard_TensorObjSubstrate.tex`, §`sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor`
38	(`lem:stalk_tensor_commutation`).
39	-/
40	
41	set_option autoImplicit false
42	
43	universe u
44	
45	open CategoryTheory Limits MonoidalCategory TopologicalSpace TopCat.Presheaf Opposite
46	
47	namespace PresheafOfModules
48	
49	open PresheafOfModules.Monoidal
50	
51	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — stalk of a tensor product (d.2) -/
52	
53	section StalkTensor
54	
55	variable {X : TopCat.{u}} {R : X.Presheaf CommRingCat.{u}}
56	  (A B : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ _ _)) (x : X)
57	
58	local notation3 "Tgt" =>
59	  TensorProduct ↑(R.stalk x) ↑(TopCat.Presheaf.stalk A.presheaf x)
60	    ↑(TopCat.Presheaf.stalk B.presheaf x)
61	
62	/-- **The `R(U)`-balanced bilinear comparison map.** For a neighbourhood `U ∋ x`, the
63	bilinear (additive-monoid-hom) map `A(U) × B(U) → A_x ⊗_{R_x} B_x` sending
64	`(a, b)` to `germ a ⊗ germ b`. Project-local: the building block of the d.2 stalk--tensor
65	comparison; Mathlib has no varying-ring stalk--tensor commutation. -/
66	noncomputable def stalkTensorBilin (U : Opens X) (hx : x ∈ U) :
67	    (↑(A.obj (op U)) : Type u) →+ (↑(B.obj (op U)) : Type u) →+ Tgt where
68	  toFun m :=
69	    ((TensorProduct.mk ↑(R.stalk x) _ _
70	      ((ConcreteCategory.hom (TopCat.Presheaf.germ A.presheaf U x hx)) m)).toAddMonoidHom).comp
71	      (ConcreteCategory.hom (TopCat.Presheaf.germ B.presheaf U x hx))
72	  map_zero' := by ext n; simp
73	  map_add' m m' := by ext n; simp
74	
75	/-- The defining balancing identity making `stalkTensorBilin` descend through the
76	relative tensor product `A(U) ⊗_{R(U)} B(U)`: `(r • a) ⊗ b ↦ a ⊗ (r • b)`. -/
77	lemma stalkTensorBilin_balanced (U : Opens X) (hx : x ∈ U) (r : ↑(R.obj (op U)))
78	    (m : ↑(A.obj (op U))) (n : ↑(B.obj (op U))) :
79	    (stalkTensorBilin A B x U hx (r • m)) n = (stalkTensorBilin A B x U hx m) (r • n) := by
80	  simp only [stalkTensorBilin, AddMonoidHom.coe_mk, ZeroHom.coe_mk, AddMonoidHom.coe_comp,
81	    LinearMap.toAddMonoidHom_coe, Function.comp_apply, TensorProduct.mk_apply]
82	  rw [PresheafOfModules.germ_smul A x U hx]
83	  erw [PresheafOfModules.germ_smul B x U hx]
84	  exact TensorProduct.smul_tmul _ _ _
85	
86	/-- **The per-neighbourhood descended comparison map** `(A ⊗ᵖ B)(U) → A_x ⊗_{R_x} B_x`,
87	obtained from `stalkTensorBilin` via `TensorProduct.liftAddHom`. The source object is
88	`(A ⊗ᵖ B).presheaf.obj (op U)`, definitionally `A(U) ⊗_{R(U)} B(U)`. -/
89	noncomputable def stalkTensorDescU (U : Opens X) (hx : x ∈ U) :
90	    (Monoidal.tensorObj A B).presheaf.obj (op U) ⟶ AddCommGrpCat.of Tgt :=
91	  AddCommGrpCat.ofHom
92	    (TensorProduct.liftAddHom (stalkTensorBilin A B x U hx) (stalkTensorBilin_balanced A B x U hx))
93	
94	@[simp] lemma stalkTensorDescU_tmul (U : Opens X) (hx : x ∈ U)
95	    (a : ↑(A.obj (op U))) (b : ↑(B.obj (op U))) :
96	    (stalkTensorDescU A B x U hx) (a ⊗ₜ[R.obj (op U)] b)
97	      = (ConcreteCategory.hom (TopCat.Presheaf.germ A.presheaf U x hx)) a ⊗ₜ[↑(R.stalk x)]
98	        (ConcreteCategory.hom (TopCat.Presheaf.germ B.presheaf U x hx)) b := by
99	  change (TensorProduct.liftAddHom (stalkTensorBilin A B x U hx)
100	    (stalkTensorBilin_balanced A B x U hx)) (a ⊗ₜ[R.obj (op U)] b) = _
101	  rw [TensorProduct.liftAddHom_tmul]
102	  rfl
103	
104	/-- **The d.2 comparison map** `(A ⊗ᵖ B).stalk x → A_x ⊗_{R_x} B_x`, obtained by descending
105	the per-neighbourhood maps `stalkTensorDescU` through the colimit defining the stalk. -/
106	noncomputable def stalkTensorDesc :
107	    TopCat.Presheaf.stalk (Monoidal.tensorObj A B).presheaf x ⟶ AddCommGrpCat.of Tgt :=
108	  colimit.desc ((OpenNhds.inclusion x).op ⋙ (Monoidal.tensorObj A B).presheaf)
109	    ⟨AddCommGrpCat.of Tgt,
110	      fun U => stalkTensorDescU A B x (unop U).1 (unop U).2,
111	      by
112	        intro U V i
113	        apply AddCommGrpCat.hom_ext
114	        ext z
115	        induction z using TensorProduct.induction_on with
116	        | zero => exact (AddMonoidHom.map_zero _).trans (AddMonoidHom.map_zero _).symm
117	        | tmul a b =>
118	            simp only [CategoryTheory.comp_apply, Functor.const_obj_map]
119	            erw [tensorObj_map_tmul, stalkTensorDescU_tmul, stalkTensorDescU_tmul]
120	            erw [germ_res_apply, germ_res_apply]
121	            rfl
122	        | add p q hp hq =>
123	            exact (AddMonoidHom.map_add _ p q).trans
124	              ((congrArg₂ HAdd.hAdd hp hq).trans (AddMonoidHom.map_add _ p q).symm)⟩
125	
126	/-- The comparison map `stalkTensorDesc` factors the per-neighbourhood map
127	`stalkTensorDescU` through the germ, by the colimit universal property. -/
128	@[reassoc]
129	lemma germ_stalkTensorDesc (U : Opens X) (hx : x ∈ U) :
130	    TopCat.Presheaf.germ (Monoidal.tensorObj A B).presheaf U x hx ≫ stalkTensorDesc A B x
131	      = stalkTensorDescU A B x U hx :=
132	  colimit.ι_desc _ (op (⟨U, hx⟩ : OpenNhds x))
133	
134	/-- **Germ characterisation of the d.2 comparison map** on a simple tensor: the germ of
135	`a ⊗ b` over `U` maps to `germ a ⊗ germ b`. -/
136	@[simp]
137	lemma stalkTensorDesc_germ_tmul (U : Opens X) (hx : x ∈ U)
138	    (a : ↑(A.obj (op U))) (b : ↑(B.obj (op U))) :
139	    (stalkTensorDesc A B x)
140	        ((ConcreteCategory.hom (TopCat.Presheaf.germ (Monoidal.tensorObj A B).presheaf U x hx))
141	          (a ⊗ₜ[R.obj (op U)] b))
142	      = (ConcreteCategory.hom (TopCat.Presheaf.germ A.presheaf U x hx)) a ⊗ₜ[↑(R.stalk x)]
143	        (ConcreteCategory.hom (TopCat.Presheaf.germ B.presheaf U x hx)) b := by
144	  rw [← stalkTensorDescU_tmul, ← CategoryTheory.comp_apply, germ_stalkTensorDesc]
145	
146	end StalkTensor
147

6	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
7	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
8	
9	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
10	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
11	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
12	\[
13	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
14	  \qquad
15	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
16	\]
17	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
18	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
19	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
20	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
21	
22	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
23	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
24	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
25	% groups)
26	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
27	% SOURCE QUOTE:
28	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
29	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
30	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
31	% abelian groups defined by the formula
32	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
33	% \end{dfn}
34	%  [\dots]
35	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
36	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
37	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
38	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
39	% topologies  by
40	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
41	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
42	%  \end{dfn}"
43	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
44	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
45	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
46	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
47	homomorphisms.}
48	
49	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
50	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
51	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
52	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
53	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
54	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
55	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
56	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
57	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
58	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
59	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
60	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
61	structure. This is the content of
62	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
63	
64	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
65	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
66	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
67	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
68	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
69	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
70	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
71	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
72	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
73	requires on the carrier is therefore only:
74	\begin{enumerate}
75	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
76	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
77	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
78	    isomorphism classes;
79	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
80	    for \(\otimes\);
81	  \item the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
82	    (\(\exists N,\ M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)) together with the three
83	    monoidal coherence isomorphisms of \(\otimes_X\): associativity
84	    \((M \otimes N) \otimes P \cong M \otimes (N \otimes P)\), unit
85	    \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes M \cong M\) (and on the right), and commutativity
86	    \(M \otimes N \cong N \otimes M\). The inverse is carried by the
87	    predicate, not by a separate lemma.
88	\end{enumerate}
89	At Mathlib's pinned commit one might be tempted to obtain (3) by instantiating a
90	\emph{full} symmetric monoidal category on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) and
91	reading the three isomorphisms off it. Two Mathlib facts make such a route
92	conceivable. First, the category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves
93	of modules over a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) is already a (symmetric)
94	monoidal category in Mathlib (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}\)),
95	with the sectionwise relative tensor \((M \otimes_R N)(U) = M(U) \otimes_{R(U)}
96	N(U)\); this applies verbatim to the \emph{varying} structure sheaf
97	\(R = \mathcal{O}_X\). Second, Mathlib's localization-of-monoidal-categories API
98	(\(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)) takes a
99	monoidal category \(C\), a localization functor \(L : C \to D\) at a morphism
100	property \(W\), and an instance \(W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), and produces a
101	\emph{full} monoidal-category structure on the localization \(D\); sheafification
102	of presheaves of modules is the localization \(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the
103	locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\), so a full monoidal structure on
104	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) would follow once \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) were
105	supplied.
106	
107	This full-monoidal-category route is, however, \emph{not} the goal, and is not
108	pursued. The goal is the commutative \emph{group} on locally-trivial iso-classes
109	(à la \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)), and that group law consumes only propositions, so
110	it never needs a coherent monoidal category --- neither the
111	pentagon/triangle/hexagon identities nor the \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance
112	that the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) API requires. What it needs is exactly the
113	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms of (3), and these are produced
114	directly: the associator by gluing canonical local isomorphisms
115	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via the single linchpin
116	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), the unitors by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\)
117	pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}), and the braiding likewise
118	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). With those three existence facts the group law
119	assembles \emph{by hand} the commutative group of \(\otimes\)-invertible
120	\(\otimes\)-iso-classes --- the Picard group
121	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) --- each group-axiom field fed a single
122	existence-of-isomorphism. This is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
123	fixed-ring \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\),
124	whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is read off the \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent
125	monoidal category \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) that
126	Mathlib supplies over a fixed ring; the present construction cannot take that
127	\(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) route, since the coherent monoidal category on
128	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) for the varying structure sheaf is exactly what it
129	avoids. The
130	\(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible},
131	Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) idiom) carries each inverse existentially.
132	The whole \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) / \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) / stalk
133	apparatus is therefore vestigial for the group law and off the critical path.
134	
135	\section{Mathlib API survey}
136	\label{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}
137	
138	The relevant pieces of the Mathlib API at the pinned commit
139	(\texttt{b80f227}) were originally assembled into a single \emph{realization route}
140	(route~(e)): obtaining a full monoidal structure on the substrate by instantiating
141	Mathlib's abstract localization-of-monoidal-categories API rather than
142	hand-assembling the associator. That route is \emph{superseded}. The group law on
143	iso-classes never consumes a coherent monoidal category, and the associator is
144	obtained by direct gluing through \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, so the
145	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance on which route~(e) hinges is never required.
146	The survey below is retained as a Mathlib-API reference and to record why the route
147	is vestigial; the four Mathlib facts are all verified against the on-disk Mathlib

242	\paragraph{The gap and route~(e), superseded.} The substrate
243	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\) of this chapter is the
244	sheafification of \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\), and
245	sheafification of presheaves of modules is exactly the localization
246	\(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\). Route~(e)
247	proposed to obtain a \emph{full} monoidal structure on
248	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\)
249	by applying \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) (ii) to the already-monoidal category (i),
250	which would require the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (its two
251	whiskering-stability fields \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}, with load-bearing
252	local-injectivity half \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}). This is
253	\emph{not} how the group law is realized. The group law consumes only the
254	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms, and the associator is built
255	directly by gluing canonical local isomorphisms through
256	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}); it never invokes
257	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), a coherent monoidal category, or the stalkwise
258	whiskering apparatus. The route~(e) instance is therefore vestigial and off the
259	critical path.
260	
261	The stalk-infrastructure analysis that route~(e) turned on is recorded here for
262	reference only, since that route is superseded. The \emph{stalk module structure}
263	is already present in Mathlib: the file
264	\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk} supplies, for a topological
265	space \(X\), a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) on \(X\) and a point
266	\(x \in X\), the \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-module structure on the stalk
267	\(\mathtt{stalk}\,M.\mathtt{presheaf}\,x\) of a presheaf of modules \(M\),
268	together with the germ--scalar compatibility \(\mathtt{germ\_smul}\); a linearity
269	packaging of the induced stalk map of a morphism was built project-side
270	(\cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}, the \emph{linearity half} of ingredient (d.1)).
271	Route~(e) additionally required a (d.1)-\emph{bridge} between the site-level
272	\(J.W\) and the topological stalkwise-isomorphism criterion on
273	\(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\), and (d.2) the commutation \((A \otimes^{p} B)_x \cong A_x
274	\otimes_{R_x} B_x\) of the stalk with the relative module-presheaf tensor; there is
275	moreover no monoidal \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\) in Mathlib. Because the group law
276	is realized through the direct-gluing associator (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso},
277	via \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}) and never through
278	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), none of this stalk apparatus is on the critical path;
279	it is retained only to document the superseded route.
280	
281	The project's policy on this gap is \emph{project-side}: the substrate is
282	built inside \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (the file
283	\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\) is its home)
284	and the consumer instance is closed against it. A parallel Mathlib upstream
285	PR with the same content is welcome but not on the critical path for the
286	project; the consumer is closed against the project-side substrate
287	independently of any upstream timing.

289	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
290	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
291	
292	\begin{definition}
293	
294	
295	\leanok
296	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
297	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
298	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
299	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
300	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
301	  \[
302	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
303	  \]
304	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
305	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
306	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
307	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
308	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
309	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
310	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
311	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
312	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
313	  canonical compatibility
314	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
315	     \;\cong\;
316	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
317	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
318	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
319	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
320	
321	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
322	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
323	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
324	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
325	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
326	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
327	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
328	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
329	\end{definition}
330	
331	\begin{lemma}
332	
333	
334	\leanok
335	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
336	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
337	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
338	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
339	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
340	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
341	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
342	  \[
343	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
344	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
345	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
346	  \]
347	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
348	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
349	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
350	  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
351	  the only datum the present lemma provides. The further coherence data
352	  of a monoidal structure --- the unitors
353	  \[
354	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
355	    \qquad
356	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
357	  \]
358	  the associator
359	  \(\alpha_{M, N, P} : (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim}
360	   M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\), and the braiding
361	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\) ---
362	  are \emph{not} outputs of this lemma; they are off the critical path,
363	  recorded in \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
364	\end{lemma}
365	
366	\begin{proof}
367	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
368	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
369	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
370	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial. The bifunctorial action
371	  on morphisms glues across the cover because the underlying gluing data of
372	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect the canonical morphisms on
373	  overlaps; equivalently, the morphism action is inherited from
374	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
375	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
376	  sheafification. The unitor/associator/braiding coherence data are
377	  \emph{not} constructed here; they are off the critical path
378	  (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
379	\end{proof}
380	
381	\begin{remark}
382	[The full monoidal category on all of $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$ is off-path; the group law consumes only propositions]
383	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
384	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
385	        lem:whisker_of_W}
386	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
387	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
388	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
389	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
390	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
391	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. This is explicitly \emph{off-path}
392	  and \emph{not pursued}. One could in principle obtain it as the output of
393	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\) applied to the
394	  already-monoidal presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\)
395	  and the sheafification localizer \(J.W\), supplying the instance
396	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}); but the group law never
397	  consumes a coherent monoidal category, so this construction is superseded and not
398	  carried out. The substrate provides only the \emph{existence} of the three
399	  coherence isomorphisms that the group law actually consumes.
400	
401	  Were that superseded route pursued, the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\)
402	  would need \emph{no} closed structure: its
403	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) field \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\) for an
404	  \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\) is the flatness-free stalkwise statement
405	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} (a \(J.W\)-morphism \(g\) is a
406	  stalkwise isomorphism, so \((F \triangleleft g)_x = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes
407	  g_x\) is again an isomorphism for any \(F\)), the same technique Mathlib uses for
408	  fixed-base presheaves in
409	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\). It would use neither
410	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) nor the
411	  sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (which is
412	  false over a non-affine open and is likewise off the critical path). But this
413	  apparatus is not on the critical path at all: the group law is realized without
414	  it.
415	
416	  Independently of how the monoidal structure is built, the relative Picard
417	  group law of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on
418	  \emph{isomorphism classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
419	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
420	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
421	  coherence data of the monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
422	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never \emph{consumed} by the
423	  group law. The group law needs only the existence of the three coherence
424	  isomorphisms \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,%
425	  lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, supplied directly: the associator by gluing canonical
426	  local isomorphisms through the restriction-compatibility isomorphism
427	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), and the
428	  unitors and braiding by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern
429	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). The carrier --- iso-classes
430	  of the substrate tensor \(\otimes_X\) --- is the scheme-theoretic analogue of
431	  Mathlib's Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(
432	  \mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}), but it
433	  is built \emph{differently}: Mathlib reads its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) off the
434	  \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent monoidal category
435	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) over a fixed ring,
436	  whereas here --- with no coherent monoidal category on
437	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) available for the varying structure sheaf --- the
438	  group is assembled by hand from the existence-of-isomorphism facts.
439	\end{remark}

441	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
442	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
443	
444	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
445	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
446	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
447	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
448	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section records the
449	\emph{existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity, unit,
450	commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law consumes. These facts
451	are supplied \emph{directly}: associativity by the direct-gluing associator
452	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} (through the linchpin
453	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), unit and commutativity by the cheap
454	\(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}),
455	and the inverse existentially from \(\otimes\)-invertibility. They are \emph{not}
456	read off any \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure: the route that would derive them from
457	\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} (route~(e)) is superseded and off-path. The section also
458	records the closure of \(\otimes\) on the locally-trivial
459	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype (\cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}) and
460	the interaction with the pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\). The open-immersion
461	restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is on the
462	critical path precisely because it is consumed by the direct-gluing associator
463	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}; it is \emph{not} tied to the superseded
464	whiskering-stability lemma \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}, which belongs
465	to the off-path route~(e).

755	[Base change along a ring isomorphism commutes with $\otimes$]
756	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
757	  \lean{restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv}
758	  Let \(e : R \simeq S\) be an isomorphism of commutative rings, and let \(M, N\) be
759	  \(S\)-modules. Restriction of scalars along \(e\) commutes with the tensor
760	  product: the canonical map
761	  \[
762	    \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M) \otimes_R \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(N)
763	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
764	    \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e\bigl(M \otimes_S N\bigr),
765	    \qquad a \otimes_R b \;\longmapsto\; a \otimes_S b,
766	  \]
767	  is an \(R\)-linear equivalence. Equivalently, \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e\) is
768	  \emph{strong} monoidal, not merely lax, when \(e\) is a ring \emph{isomorphism}:
769	  base change along an isomorphism \(R \simeq S\) identifies the two ground rings, so
770	  the lax structure map of \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\) is invertible. The
771	  forward map is \(R\)-linear, but its inverse \(a \otimes_S b \mapsto a \otimes_R b\)
772	  is only \emph{additive} (it is not \(S\)-linear), so the inverse is constructed as

900	[Flat whiskering preserves the sheafification localizer]
901	  \label{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}
902	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
903	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a Grothendieck
904	  topology \(J\), and let \(J.W\) be the class of morphisms of presheaves of
905	  \(R\)-modules that are inverted by sheafification --- equivalently, the
906	  \emph{locally bijective} morphisms, i.e.\ those that are both locally injective
907	  and locally surjective for \(J\) (the characterisation
908	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\)). Let \(F\) be a presheaf of \(R\)-modules
909	  that is \emph{flat} (sectionwise: \(F(U)\) is a flat \(R(U)\)-module for every
910	  object \(U\) of the site). Then for every morphism \(g\) in \(J.W\) the
911	  left-whiskered morphism
912	  \[
913	    F \triangleleft g \;\;=\;\; \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g
914	  \]
915	  (the action of \(F \otimes^{p} -\) on \(g\), with \(\otimes^{p}\) the
916	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product) again lies in \(J.W\). By the symmetry of
917	  the presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, the right-whiskered morphism
918	  \(g \triangleright F\) likewise lies in \(J.W\) when \(F\) is flat.
919	
920	  Both halves --- the left-whiskering statement and its braiding-conjugate
921	  right-whiskering statement --- are established. They are valid standalone
922	  results about flat whiskering of sheafification-localizer morphisms. They are,
923	  however, \emph{off the critical path}: the live whiskering-stability obligation
924	  for the unconditional associator is closed for an \emph{arbitrary} whiskering
925	  object \(F\) by the stalk--tensor commutation
926	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}
927	  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor}), which never invokes the
928	  sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of the present lemma. The sectionwise flatness
929	  condition --- \(F(U)\) flat over \(R(U)\) for \emph{every} object \(U\) of the
930	  site, including non-affine opens --- is not supplied by \(\otimes\)-invertibility
931	  or local triviality, which are affine-local properties; the present (flat) lemma
932	  is therefore retained as an independent, closed result, superseded on the critical
933	  path by the d.2 route of \cref{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor}.
934	\end{lemma}
935	
936	\begin{proof}
937	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
938	  By the locally-bijective characterisation of the sheafification localizer
939	  (\(J.W\) is exactly the class of morphisms that are \(J\)-locally injective and
940	  \(J\)-locally surjective), it suffices to show that \(F \triangleleft g\) is
941	  both locally injective and locally surjective whenever \(g\) is.
942	
943	  The presheaf-of-modules tensor product is computed sectionwise,
944	  \((F \otimes^{p} G)(U) = F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} G(U)\), so for
945	  \(g : A \to B\) the whiskered morphism acts on sections over \(U\) as the
946	  \(R(U)\)-linear map
947	  \[
948	    \mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U) \;\colon\;
949	      F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} A(U) \;\longrightarrow\; F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U).
950	  \]
951	
952	  \emph{Local surjectivity} is automatic and needs no hypothesis on \(F\):
953	  tensoring is right exact, so applying \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the cokernel
954	  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\); since \(g\) is locally
955	  surjective its cokernel is locally zero, hence so is the cokernel of
956	  \(F \triangleleft g\), and \(F \triangleleft g\) is locally surjective.
957	
958	  \emph{Local injectivity} is where flatness enters. Because each \(F(U)\) is a
959	  flat \(R(U)\)-module, the functor \(F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} -\) preserves injective
960	  linear maps (Mathlib
961	  \(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\)). Thus
962	  whenever \(g(U)\) is injective on a covering sieve, so is
963	  \((\mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U))\); the local injectivity of \(g\) is
964	  therefore transported sectionwise to \(F \triangleleft g\).
965	
966	  Locally injective and locally surjective together give
967	  \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). The right-whiskered statement
968	  \(g \triangleright F\) follows by conjugating \(F \triangleleft g\) with the
969	  braiding of the symmetric presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, which is an
970	  isomorphism and hence preserves membership in \(J.W\); the flatness of \(F\) is
971	  the only input, and it is symmetric in the two tensor factors.
972	\end{proof}
